schools.summary: Gill: the Targums render it the sacrifice of holy things, or of thanksgivings; the peace offering of the herd might be male or female, but without blemish; the witnesses note God appointing the peace offering of the herd to be male or female, without blemish, offered before the LORD — the peace offering, the sacrifice of fellowship and thanksgiving, freely accepted (the offering of peace male or female, but unblemished: 'if his oblation be a sacrifice of peace offering, if he offer it of the herd; whether it be a male or female, he shall offer it without blemish'; the kind, 'a sacrifice of peace offering,' rendered by the Targums the sacrifice of holy things or of thanksgivings, the offering of peace, fellowship, and thankful praise, in which the offerer shared and feasted before God; the latitude, 'whether it be a male or female,' that, unlike the burnt offering, male only, the peace offering might be of either sex, a greater freedom in this offering of communion; the requirement, 'without blemish before the LORD,' that, though either sex, it must be perfect and unblemished, a type of Christ the Lamb without blemish; the type the commentators draw, that the peace offering shadows Christ our peace, by whom we have reconciliation and communion with God, and the believer's fellowship and thanksgiving; that in Christ God meets his people in peace).

debates.question: What does the peace offering shadow?

sides.position: Christ our peace (Ephesians 2:14) — by whom we have reconciliation and communion with God — and the believer's fellowship and thanksgiving; the offering of peace, in which the offerer shared and feasted before God, a sacrifice of communion (allowing male or female, a greater freedom than the burnt offering)

themes: if his oblation be a sacrifice of peace offering... whether it be a male or female
he shall offer it without blemish before the LORD
the peace offering of the herd, male or female but unblemished, the sacrifice of fellowship and thanksgiving
a type of Christ our peace, by whom we have reconciliation and communion with God, in whom God meets his people in peace

schools.summary: Gill: he laid his hand, his right hand with strength, the Targum of Jonathan says, on the head of the offering, killed it at the door, and the priests sprinkled the blood round about the altar; the witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the peace offering, killing it at the door, and the priests sprinkling the blood round the altar — the laying on of hands and the sprinkled blood, the same atoning ground for the peace offering (the peace offering too resting on the substitute and the blood: 'he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it at the door... and... the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about'; the laying on, 'lay his hand upon the head,' the offerer identifying himself with the victim, as in the burnt offering; the slaying, 'kill it at the door of the tabernacle,' the victim put to death in the appointed place; the blood, 'the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about,' the blood applied for atonement; the great truth the commentators draw, that even the peace offering, the offering of fellowship, rests on atonement, for there is no peace with God but by the blood of the substitute; that our fellowship and communion with God are grounded on the atoning death of Christ; that peace is made by the blood of his cross).

themes: he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation
and Aaron's sons the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about
the offerer laying his hand on the peace offering, killing it, and the priests sprinkling its blood
even the offering of fellowship resting on atonement; no peace with God but by the blood of the substitute, peace made by the blood of his cross

schools.summary: Gill: the priest offered, not all of it, but some of it, even the fat that covereth the inwards and all the fat upon the inwards, an offering made by fire to the LORD; the witnesses note the priest offering by fire the fat that covered the inwards and all the fat upon them — the giving of the fat, the best and richest, to God, while the rest was shared (the fat God's portion in the peace offering: 'he shall offer of the sacrifice of the peace offering an offering made by fire... the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards'; that not all the victim, but a part, was burnt to God, the rest being shared, the breast and shoulder to the priests, the rest to the offerer for the feast; the part for God, 'the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards,' the rich fat, the choicest and best, given to God; the principle, that the fat is the LORD's, the best of the offering reserved for him; the meaning the commentators draw, that the fat, the richest part, and the inward fat, the inward affections and the best of the heart, are to be given to God, the inmost and the choicest devoted to him; that in our fellowship with God the best and the inward is his).

themes: he shall offer of the sacrifice of the peace offering an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat that covereth the inwards
and all the fat that is upon the inwards
the fat, the best and richest, offered by fire to the LORD while the rest was shared
the fat being God's portion; some seeing the inward fat signify the inward affections and the best of the heart given to God

schools.summary: Gill: the two kidneys next the flanks, with the fat on them, and the caul above the liver, were taken away to be burnt; the witnesses note God appointing the two kidneys, the fat on them, and the caul above the liver to be taken away and offered — the choice inward parts, with the fat, set apart for God (the kidneys and the caul, with their fat, God's: 'the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them... and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away'; the parts, 'the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them,' and 'the caul above the liver,' the choice inner parts, with their rich fat, taken away from the rest; that these, with the fat, were the portion burnt to God, the best and inmost of the victim; the meaning some draw, that the kidneys, the reins, the seat of the inward affections and desires, the reins God tries, and the caul above the liver, with the fat, signify the inmost affections and the richest of the heart, given to God; that God claims the inward parts, the secret affections and desires of the soul; that in true fellowship the heart and its desires are given to God).

themes: the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys
it shall he take away
the two kidneys, their fat, and the caul above the liver taken away and offered to God
some seeing the kidneys (the reins, the seat of the inward affections) signify the inmost affections of the heart, God claiming the secret desires of the soul

schools.summary: Gill: the fat of the several parts was burnt on the altar upon the burnt offering, signifying that it was acceptable to God, an offering of a sweet savour; the witnesses note the priests burning the fat on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice, a sweet savour to the LORD — the burning of the fat upon the burnt offering, well-pleasing to God (the peace offering's fat burnt with the daily burnt offering, a sweet savour: 'Aaron's sons shall burn it on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice... an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD'; the burning, 'burn it on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice,' that the fat of the peace offering was laid and burnt upon the burnt offering, the continual sacrifice on the altar, the two going up together; the acceptance, 'of a sweet savour unto the LORD,' that the fat, the best, burnt to God, was well-pleasing and accepted; the meaning the commentators draw, that the peace offering, the offering of fellowship, rested upon and was joined with the burnt offering of entire devotion, our communion with God grounded on and rising with the offering of Christ; that our peace and fellowship with God are accepted only as resting on the all-devoted sacrifice; that the best given to God in fellowship is a sweet savour).

themes: Aaron's sons shall burn it on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice, which is upon the wood that is on the fire
it is an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD
the fat of the peace offering burnt upon the burnt sacrifice, the two going up together, a sweet savour
the offering of fellowship resting on the burnt offering of entire devotion; our peace accepted only as resting on the all-devoted sacrifice of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the peace offering might be of the flock, and be either male or female, but it must be without blemish; the witnesses note God appointing the peace offering of the flock to be male or female, without blemish — the lesser peace offering of the flock, the same in kind (the flock-offering of peace, like the herd, of either sex, but unblemished: 'if his offering for a sacrifice of peace offering... be of the flock; male or female, he shall offer it without blemish'; the kind, 'of the flock,' a smaller offering than the herd, for those of less means; the latitude, 'male or female,' that, as with the herd-offering, the peace offering of the flock might be of either sex, the greater freedom of the offering of communion; the requirement, 'without blemish,' that, though either sex, it must be perfect and unblemished, a type of Christ the Lamb without blemish; that God made room for the peace offering of every estate, the same fellowship open to the poorer sort; the type before given, that the peace offering shadows Christ our peace, by whom all his people, rich or poor, have communion with God; that the same peace is offered in great and small).

themes: if his offering for a sacrifice of peace offering unto the LORD be of the flock; male or female
he shall offer it without blemish
the peace offering of the flock, male or female but unblemished, for those of less means
the same fellowship open to the poorer sort; a type of Christ our peace, by whom all his people, rich or poor, have communion with God

schools.summary: Gill: if he offer a lamb, which was of the flock and must be of the first year; this is a rule, that wherever this word is used in the law, it signifies one of the first year; the witnesses note God appointing that, if a lamb be offered for the peace offering, it be brought before the LORD — the bringing of the lamb, of the first year, before God (the lamb offered in God's presence: 'If he offer a lamb for his offering, then shall he offer it before the LORD'; the victim, 'a lamb,' of the flock, of the first year, young and perfect; the place, 'before the LORD,' that it was brought into God's presence, at the appointed place, to be offered; the type the commentators draw, that the lamb, the young and unblemished victim, shadows Christ, the Lamb of God, the Lamb slain, offered before God for his people; that the lamb was ever a chief type of Christ, the meek and spotless victim; that the peace offering, of a lamb, points to the peace made by the Lamb of God).

themes: If he offer a lamb for his offering
then shall he offer it before the LORD
the lamb of the first year offered for the peace offering before the LORD
a type of Christ, the Lamb of God, offered before God for his people; the peace offering of a lamb pointing to the peace made by the Lamb of God

schools.summary: Gill: the Targum of Jonathan adds, his right hand with strength, on the head of the offering, the same as with the herd; the witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the lamb, killing it before the tabernacle, and the priests sprinkling its blood round the altar — the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the lamb as for the herd (the lamb too resting on the substitute and the blood: 'he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it before the tabernacle... and Aaron's sons shall sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar'; the laying on, 'lay his hand upon the head,' the offerer identifying with the victim, the sin transferred, as before; the slaying, 'kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation,' the lamb put to death in God's presence; the blood, 'sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar,' applied for atonement, as with the herd; that the same order and the same atoning ground held for the lamb, the peace and fellowship resting on the blood of the substitute; the type before given, that our peace with God is grounded on the death and blood of Christ, the Lamb; that there is no fellowship with God but through atonement).

themes: he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation
and Aaron's sons shall sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar
the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the lamb as for the herd
our peace with God grounded on the death and blood of Christ the Lamb, no fellowship with God but through atonement

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the whole rump, the fat tail, was taken off hard by the backbone; the eastern sheep have large, fat tails, sometimes weighing many pounds, accounted a great delicacy; the witnesses note God appointing the fat and the whole rump, the fat tail, of the lamb to be taken off and offered, with the fat covering the inwards — the giving of the fat and the rich fat-tail of the lamb to God (the fat and the whole rump God's: 'the fat thereof, and the whole rump, it shall he take off hard by the backbone; and the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards'; the rump, 'the whole rump... hard by the backbone,' the large fat tail of the eastern sheep, accounted a great delicacy, the richest part of the lamb, here given to God; the fat, 'the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat,' the inward fat also, as with the herd; that the best and richest of the lamb, the fat and the fat-tail, were burnt to God, the choicest reserved for him; the meaning before given, that the fat, the best, given to God, signifies the best and the inward devoted to him; that God claims the choicest in our fellowship).

themes: the fat thereof, and the whole rump, it shall he take off hard by the backbone
and the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards
the fat and the whole rump (the rich fat tail of the eastern sheep) of the lamb offered to the LORD
the best and richest of the lamb burnt to God, the choicest reserved for him, the best and the inward devoted

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the same direction is given here as about the bullock of the peace offering, the kidneys, their fat, and the caul above the liver taken away; the witnesses note God appointing the two kidneys, the fat on them, and the caul above the liver of the lamb to be taken away and offered — the same choice inward parts of the lamb set apart for God, as of the herd (the kidneys and the caul, with their fat, God's: 'the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them... and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away'; the parts, 'the two kidneys... and the caul above the liver,' the same inner parts as in the herd-offering, with their rich fat; that the same direction was given for the lamb as for the bullock, the same portion taken for God; the meaning before given, that the kidneys, the reins, the seat of the inward affections, and the caul, with the fat, signify the inmost affections and the richest of the heart given to God; that God claims the inward and the best in the lamb as in the bullock, in the lesser offering as in the greater; that the heart and its desires are given to God).

themes: the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys
it shall he take away
the two kidneys, their fat, and the caul above the liver of the lamb taken away and offered, as of the herd
God claiming the inward and the best in the lamb as in the bullock; the kidneys signifying the inmost affections of the heart given to God

schools.summary: Gill: the priest burnt the fat of the tail, of the inwards, the two kidneys, and the caul of the liver, called the food of the offering made by fire to the LORD; the witnesses note the priest burning the fat upon the altar, called the food of the offering made by fire to the LORD — the fat as God's food, the portion of the offering that is his (the fat the bread or food of the offering: 'the priest shall burn it upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD'; the burning, 'the priest shall burn it upon the altar,' that the fat, the rump, the kidneys, and the caul were all burnt to God; the striking phrase, 'the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD,' that the fat burnt to God is called his food or bread, spoken after the manner of men, as the portion of the offering reserved for God, his table furnished from the altar; the meaning the commentators draw, that God speaks of the offering as his food, not as needing it, but to mark it as his portion, given to him and accepted; the type, that Christ's offering is the bread of God's table, the satisfaction in which God is well-pleased, and that the sacrifice furnishes both God's portion and the believer's feast; that the best, burnt to God, is his food and delight).

debates.question: What is meant by the fat being 'the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD'?

sides.position: Spoken after the manner of men — not that God needs it (Psalm 50:12-13), but to mark the fat burnt to God as his portion, his 'food' or 'bread,' given to him and accepted; a type that Christ's offering is the bread of God's table, the satisfaction in which God is well-pleased, the sacrifice furnishing both God's portion and the believer's feast

themes: And the priest shall burn it upon the altar
it is the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD
the fat burnt upon the altar, called the food of the offering made by fire to the LORD
the fat as God's 'food,' spoken after the manner of men to mark it his portion; Christ's offering the bread of God's table, in which he is well-pleased

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'a goat,' implying the whole species, he-goat, she-goat, and kid; the witnesses note God appointing that, if the peace offering be a goat, it be offered before the LORD — the goat of the flock for the peace offering, the third kind (the goat too offered: 'if his offering be a goat, then he shall offer it before the LORD'; the kind, 'a goat,' of the flock, implying the whole species, he-goat, she-goat, and kid, another sort of peace offering; the place, 'before the LORD,' that it was brought into God's presence, at the appointed place; that the peace offering might be of the herd, bullock, or of the flock, lamb or goat, God making room for various offerings according to men's substance; the type before given, that all these clean victims shadow Christ, the one true peace offering, of whom all spoke; that the same fellowship and peace were open through every kind of offering).

themes: And if his offering be a goat
then he shall offer it before the LORD
the peace offering of a goat (the whole species) offered before the LORD, the third kind
God making room for various offerings according to men's substance, all shadowing Christ, the one true peace offering

schools.summary: Gill: the same directions are given for the killing of it and the sprinkling of its blood as in the offerings of the bullock and lamb; the witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the goat, killing it before the tabernacle, and the priests sprinkling its blood round the altar — the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the goat as for the rest (the goat too resting on the substitute and the blood: 'he shall lay his hand upon the head of it, and kill it before the tabernacle... and the sons of Aaron shall sprinkle the blood thereof upon the altar round about'; the laying on, 'lay his hand upon the head,' the offerer identifying with the victim, the sin transferred, as before; the slaying and the blood, 'kill it... and... sprinkle the blood... round about,' the same directions as in the offerings of the bullock and lamb, the victim slain and its blood applied for atonement; that the same atoning ground held for every kind of peace offering, the fellowship resting on the blood of the substitute; the type before given, that our peace with God is grounded on the death and blood of Christ; that there is one way of peace, by the blood of the substitute, in every offering).

themes: he shall lay his hand upon the head of it, and kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation
and the sons of Aaron shall sprinkle the blood thereof upon the altar round about
the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the goat as for the bullock and lamb
the same atoning ground for every kind of peace offering; one way of peace by the blood, our peace grounded on the death of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the same rules are laid down about taking the fat off the several parts as in the sacrifice of the bullock; but nothing is said of the fat of the rump and tail, as is said of the lamb; the witnesses note God appointing the fat that covered the inwards and all the fat upon them of the goat to be offered by fire — the giving of the fat of the goat to God, as of the rest, though without the fat-tail (the fat of the goat too God's: 'he shall offer thereof his offering... the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards'; the parts, 'the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat,' the same inward fat as in the bullock; the difference noted, that nothing is said of the fat of the rump and tail, as with the lamb, the goat having no such fat-tail; that, save for that, the same rules held, the fat and the inward parts given to God; the meaning before given, that the fat, the best, given to God, signifies the best and the inward devoted to him; that God claims the choicest in every kind of offering).

themes: he shall offer thereof his offering, even an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat that covereth the inwards
and all the fat that is upon the inwards
the fat that covered the inwards of the goat offered by fire to the LORD, the same as the bullock
the fat, the best, given to God signifying the best and the inward devoted to him; God claiming the choicest in every kind of offering

schools.summary: Gill: this shows that not the fat only, but the inwards and the kidneys, were burnt also; the priest salted the parts and burned them on the altar, for all the fat is the LORD's; the witnesses note the priest burning the fat and the inward parts on the altar, the food of the offering made by fire for a sweet savour, for all the fat is the LORD's — the burning of the fat as God's portion and the great rule, all the fat is his (all the fat belonging to God: 'the priest shall burn them upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire for a sweet savour: all the fat is the LORD's'; the burning, 'burn them upon the altar,' that not the fat only, but the inwards and the kidneys were burnt, salted and laid on the altar; the food, 'the food of the offering... for a sweet savour,' the fat as God's portion, well-pleasing and accepted; the great rule, 'all the fat is the LORD's,' that the fat, the best and richest, in every offering, belonged to God, reserved wholly for him; the meaning the commentators draw, that God claims the best of all, the fat and the inward, the choicest of our substance and the inmost of our hearts; that the best is to be given to God, not kept for ourselves; that what is wholly God's is a sweet savour).

debates.question: What does 'all the fat is the LORD's' teach?

sides.position: That the fat, the best and richest in every offering, belonged wholly to God — God claiming the best of all, the choicest of our substance and the inmost of our hearts (the fat and the inward parts); the best to be given to God, not kept for ourselves

themes: the priest shall burn them upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire for a sweet savour
all the fat is the LORD's
the fat and inward parts burnt on the altar, the food of the offering, a sweet savour
'all the fat is the LORD's' — the best and richest in every offering belonging wholly to God, who claims the choicest of our substance and the inmost of our hearts

schools.summary: Gill: it was a perpetual statute, unto the end of the Mosaic dispensation, until the Messiah comes and his sacrifice is offered and his blood is shed; throughout all their dwellings, that they eat neither fat nor blood; the witnesses note God making it a perpetual statute, in all their generations and dwellings, that they eat neither fat nor blood — the lasting law reserving the fat and the blood from common use (fat and blood God's, not man's food: 'a perpetual statute for your generations throughout all your dwellings, that ye eat neither fat nor blood'; the perpetuity, 'a perpetual statute for your generations,' binding unto the end of the Mosaic dispensation, until the Messiah comes and his blood is shed, in all generations and throughout all your dwellings; the fat, that the fat was forbidden as food because it was the LORD's portion, reserved for God; the blood, that the blood was forbidden because the life of the flesh is in the blood, and the blood was given to make atonement, too sacred for common use; the great meaning the commentators draw, that the fat, the best, and the blood, the life and the means of atonement, were kept sacred to God, the blood pointing to the atoning blood of Christ, not to be profaned; that the law taught a holy reverence for the blood, the price of atonement; that the things of God are to be held sacred).

debates.question: Why were fat and blood forbidden as food?

sides.position: The fat because it was the LORD's portion, reserved for God; the blood because 'the life of the flesh is in the blood' and the blood was given to make atonement (Leviticus 17:11), too sacred for common use — pointing to the atoning blood of Christ, not to be profaned, teaching a holy reverence for the blood, the price of atonement

themes: a perpetual statute for your generations throughout all your dwellings
that ye eat neither fat nor blood
the perpetual statute reserving the fat (the LORD's portion) and the blood from common use
the blood forbidden because it makes atonement, pointing to the atoning blood of Christ, not to be profaned; a holy reverence for the blood, the price of atonement
